[10]   K. Hinterbichler and J. Khoury,Symmetron Fields: Screening Scalar Forces Through Local Symmetry Breaking,Phys. Rev. Lett. 104, 231301 (2010).

[11]   A. I. Vainshtein, To the problem of nonvanishing graviton mass, Phys. Lett. B 39, 393 (1972).

[12]   C. Deffayet, G. Esposito-Farčse, and A. Vikman, Covariant Galileon, Phys. Rev. D 79, 084003 (2009).

[13]   E. Babichev, C. Deffayet, and R. Ziour, The Vainshtein mechanism in the Decoupling Limit of massive gravity, JHEP 2009, 098 (2009).

[14]   T. Clifton, P. G. Ferreira, A. Padilla, and C. Skordis, Modified gravity and cosmology, Phys. Rep. 513, 1 (2012).

[15]   S. Hossenfelder, A covariant version of Verlinde’s emergent gravity, Phys. Rev. D 95, 124018 (2017).

[16]   Alexandre Barreira, Philippe Brax, Sebastien Clesse, Baojiu Li, Patrick Valageas, K-mouflage gravity models that pass Solar System and cosmological constraints, Phys. Rev. D 91, 024033 (2015).

[17]   M. Milgrom, "A modification of the Newtonian dynamics as a possible alternative to the hidden mass hypothesis", Astrophys. J. 270, 365 (1983).

Part XIII
Part 13. Quantum Field IR Balance of the Vacuum and Renormalization of the Geometric Component of Gravity

Abstract. The quantum field energy balance of the vacuum in a closed Universe and the mechanism of renormalization of the geometric component of gravity within the framework of the infrared-ultraviolet interconnection concept are investigated. The local vacuum energy density is treated as a dynamically bounded quantity determined by the global causal scale of the event horizon. By calculating the quantum contribution of matter fields in the one-loop approximation, it is established that the predominance of fermionic degrees of freedom and the specificity of the mass hierarchy of elementary particles lead to a negative value for the net field vacuum pressure. It is shown that the complete exclusion of an independent static cosmological constant from the description shifts the energy budget of the Universe into the form of a self-consistent constraint, where the negative quantum dominance is compensated by collective effects in the lightest fermionic sector. The mechanism of energy sign inversion due to the transition of the specified sector into a macroscopic coherent bosonic state of a vacuum fluid or due to coupling with an ultra-light scalar field at the infrared freezing point of the renormalization group flow is described. The developed approach allows analytically determining the mass scale of light fermions directly from the event horizon radius and substantiates the stability of the vacuum balance without involving mechanisms of fine-tuning constants.

0.1 Global Invariants of a Closed Universe

The total energy density of the vacuum of the Universe ρvactotal is a conserving Noether invariant. It is rigidly tied to the total mass of the Universe M0. The average vacuum energy density in a homogeneous space is defined as the ratio of the total mass to the geometric volume of the three-dimensional sphere V bounded by the limiting causal horizon radius L:

ρvactotal = M0 V = M0 4 3πL3 = 3M0 4πL3 		(1)

Within this paradigm, the mass of the Universe M0 is completely determined by the classical black hole (Schwarzschild) formula for closed systems. This fundamental connection is strictly satisfied for the de Sitter Universe and determines the fundamental IR/UV interconnection (infrared-ultraviolet connection) between the local quantum vacuum energy density (UV) and the maximum size of the cosmological event horizon (IR), setting a holographic threshold for effective field theory [5].

By virtue of the linear topological identity of the Planck geometry of the model, the total mass of the Universe and its limiting horizon radius are connected by a strict relationship:

M0 = L 		(2)

Substituting this identity into the density expression leads to the cancellation of spatial powers in the numerator and denominator:

ρvactotal = 3 ⋅ (L) 4πL3 = 3 4πL2 		(3)

Thus, its exact value is expressed through the inverse square of the limiting causal horizon radius of the Universe L. The obtained expression sets the fundamental UV/IR interaction (ultraviolet-infrared balance), connecting the local quantum vacuum energy density (UV) with the global geometric scale of the horizon of the Universe (IR) [5].

Definitions of global variables:

  • M0 — the total conserving mass of the Universe in Planck units.

  • L — the limiting causal horizon radius of the Universe in Planck units.

  • ρvactotal — the total energy density of the vacuum of the Universe in Planck units.

—

0.2 Coleman–Weinberg Effective Potential Equation and One-Loop QFT Balance

To calculate the quantum contribution of matter fields to the total vacuum energy density of the Universe, the method of the Coleman–Weinberg effective potential in the one-loop approximation is used [1]. Ultraviolet divergences of the vacuum zero-point oscillations are eliminated by introducing counterterms in the modified minimal subtraction (MSŻ) scheme [2].

According to the principles of the functional renormalization group (FRG), in bounded volumes, the continuous Wetterich flow experiences an infrared (IR) cutoff at a scale set by the geometry of the system [4]. In the model under consideration, this wave mode cutoff threshold is rigidly fixed by the limiting causal horizon radius of the Universe L, which determines the renormalization scale as μ = 1∕L.

The Standard Model, with its internal gauge and chiral symmetries, must generate a total zero-point oscillation energy that is completely determined and bounded by the black hole formula through the total vacuum budget [5]. The total energy density of the vacuum of the Universe is composed of the geometric component of gravity Λgeom and the total energy of the Standard Model fields in the one-loop Coleman–Weinberg approximation ρ1-loopSM:

Λgeom + ρ1-loopSM = 3 4πL2 		(4)

Taking into account the IR cutoff scale μ = 1∕L, the quantum contribution of the Standard Model ρ1-loopSM expands over three independent spin sectors of fields based on the canonical QFT subtraction apparatus [1,2]:

ρ1-loopSM = 1 64π2 [Σh + ΣV -ΣF total ] 		(5)

where the functional contributions for each field contain real, finite infrared particle masses mi and strictly defined coefficients:

0.2.1 Real Scalar Higgs Field (h)

  • Number of degrees of freedom: 1 (one real component).

  • Subtraction scheme constant: 3∕2.

Σh = 1 ⋅ mh4 (ln ⁡ (m h2L2) -3 2 ) 		(6)

0.2.2 Gauge Vector Bosons (Massive Electroweak Fields)

  • Subtraction scheme constant for massive vector fields: 5∕6.

  • Neutral gauge boson Z0: has 3 spatial spin projections.

ΣZ = 3 ⋅ mZ4 (ln ⁡ (m Z2L2) -5 6 ) 		(7)

  • Charged gauge bosons W+ and W-: have 3 spin projections each, totaling 6 degrees of freedom.

ΣW = 6 ⋅ mW 4 (ln ⁡ (m W 2L2) -5 6 ) 		(8)

The total contribution of gauge bosons is ΣV = ΣZ + ΣW . Massless photons and gluons do not contribute.

0.2.3 Fermionic Fields (Quarks and Leptons of the Standard Model)

  • Subtraction scheme constant for all fermions: 3∕2.

  • For each quark, the number of degrees of freedom is 12 (3 colors × 2 spin projections × 2 to account for antiparticles).

  • For each charged lepton, the number of degrees of freedom is 4 (2 spin projections × 2 to account for antiparticles).

  • For each neutrino, only left-handed components are considered; the number of degrees of freedom is 2 (1 spin projection × 2 to account for antiparticles).

The full fermionic contribution ΣF total consists of the sum of the contributions of all three generations of fundamental fermions:

Quark subsector:

Σquarks = 12mt4 (ln ⁡ (m t2L2) -3 2 ) + 12mb4 (ln ⁡ (m b2L2) -3 2 ) + 12mc4 (ln ⁡ (m c2L2) -3 2 ) + 12ms4 (ln ⁡ (m s2L2) -3 2 ) + 12mu4 (ln ⁡ (m u2L2) -3 2 ) + 12md4 (ln ⁡ (m d2L2) -3 2 ) (9)

Charged lepton subsector:

Σleptons = 4mτ4 (ln ⁡ (m τ2L2) -3 2 ) + 4mμ4 (ln ⁡ (m μ2L2) -3 2 ) + 4me4 (ln ⁡ (m e2L2) -3 2 ) 		(10)

Neutrino subsector:

Σneutrinos = 2mνe4 (ln ⁡ (m νe2L2) -3 2 ) + 2mνμ4 (ln ⁡ (m νμ2L2) -3 2 ) + 2mντ4 (ln ⁡ (m ντ2L2) -3 2 ) 		(11)

ΣF total = Σ quarks + Σleptons + Σneutrinos 		(12)

Definitions of local field variables:

  • mh,mZ,mW ,mt,mb,mc,ms,mu,md,mτ,mμ,me,mνe,mνμ,mντ — real, finite infrared masses of the corresponding Standard Model particles in Planck units.

—

0.3 Dominance of the Fermionic Sector and the IR Sign of the Quantum Contribution

The physical sign of the final quantum contribution ρ1-loopSM is determined by the internal balance between the bosonic and fermionic terms [1]. In the deep IR limit, the sign of the difference [Σh + ΣV -ΣF total ] is rigidly fixed by two factors: the number of internal degrees of freedom of the Standard Model fields and the hierarchy of their real physical masses.

The total number of degrees of freedom of all Standard Model fermions is gF = 90, while the total number of degrees of freedom of all massive SM bosons (including the Higgs) is gB = 10. The total number of quantum fermionic modes exceeds the bosonic ones in a 9:1 ratio. Since the loop functionals Σi are proportional to the fourth power of the particle masses ( ~ mi4), the primary weight of the fermionic sector is concentrated in the top quark (t), which possesses the maximum physical rest mass among all SM fields. The predominance of the fermionic component causes the difference inside the square brackets of the effective potential equation to become a strictly negative quantity:

(Σh + ΣV -ΣF total ) < 0 		(13)

Consequently, the net one-loop contribution of the Standard Model fields to the vacuum energy density is negative: ρ1-loopSM < 0.

0.3.1 Quantitative Analysis and Hierarchy of the Leading Terms

Direct calculation of the contributions in Planck units reveals a rigid hierarchy of fields, where an exclusive role is played by the four most massive particles of the Standard Model. The dimensionless arguments of the logarithms ln ⁡ (mi2L2) for them have the same scale and amount to a value of the order of ≈ 195. The leading terms of the equation are distributed across the following orders of magnitude:

  • Top quark (t): Σt ≈ +9.65 × 10-65

  • Massive gauge bosons (Z0,W±): ΣV ≈ +4.14 × 10-66

  • Higgs field (h): Σh ≈ +2.22 × 10-66

Substitution of the leading terms into the formula fixes the final order of the QFT vacuum:

ρ1-loopSM = 1 64π2 ((2.22 × 10-66) + (4.14 × 10-66) - (9.65 × 10-65) ) ≈-1.43 × 10-67 		(14)

The quantum fermionic pressure of the Standard Model matter fields ( - 1.43 × 10-67) exceeds the final Noether vacuum budget of the Universe ( + 2.39 × 10-123) by exactly 56 orders of magnitude. To compensate for the fermionic quantum pressure of the SM and maintain the total vacuum energy density at the positive Noether level 3 4πL2, the geometric horizon term must take a strictly positive and excess value:

Λgeom = 3 4πL2 - ρ1-loopSM = 3 4πL2 + |ρ1-loopSM|≈ +1.43 × 10-67 		(15)

0.4 Exclusion of Λgeom and Dynamic Regeneration of the Residual Vacuum via the Neutrino Sector

Within the framework of the ideal quantum-informational paradigm, we completely exclude the independent geometric parameter from the action, setting Λgeom ≡ 0. The entire observed vacuum budget of the Universe is converted into the form of a dynamic self-consistent constraint, where the macroscopic fermionic QFT dominance of the top quark ( ~-10-67) is compensated by the self-interaction constants of emergent fields [5]:

ρ1-loopSM\ν + ρ emergent + ρνeffective = 3 4πL2 		(16)

Here, ρ1-loopSM\ν is the negative loop contribution of all SM fields excluding neutrinos. By itself, the free fermionic loop of neutrinos within a standard QFT calculation also has a negative sign. However, in both models considered (Wetterich’s cosmon [4] and the Cooper pairs [3]), the residue 3 4πL2 is entirely attributed precisely to the neutrino sector, since due to the IR interaction with the macroscopic background, the sign of the neutrino contribution physically inverts to positive on the basis of the holographic UV/IR isomorphism of a black hole [5].

0.4.1 Self-Interaction in the Cosmon Model (Dilatonic Quintessence)

Upon the exclusion of Λgeom, the role of the compensation buffer is assumed by the vacuum expectation value of the ultra-light scalar field — the cosmon (χ) at the IR freezing point [4]. The effective cosmon potential V (χ) is expressed through its dimensionless quantum self-interaction coefficient λχ (the quartic coupling constant at the IR-fixed point of the renormalization group) and the vacuum expectation value of the field ⟨χ⟩freezing:

ρemergent ≡ V (χfreezing) = 1 4λχ⟨χ⟩freezing4 		(17)

Mechanism of sign inversion: The renormalization group flow shows that when the cosmon rolls down to the IR point, the neutrino masses dynamically grow, depending on the field χ [4]. This gives rise to an inverse elastic reaction of the vacuum — a cosmon-neutrino coupling. At the IR stopping point of the flow on the horizon (μ = 1∕L), the negative contribution of the top quark and the positive self-interaction potential of the cosmon compensate for each other up to the 56th decimal place. The quantum subtraction is structured such that the cosmon overcompensates for the top quark, and due to the strong coupling of the field χ with neutrino currents, the residual energy difference is pumped into collective binding. The sign of the contribution becomes strictly positive, and the scale of the residue is rigidly dictated by the neutrino parameters. The full vacuum energy balance equation takes the form of a self-consistent equation for the IR self-interaction coefficient of the cosmon:

1 4λχ⟨χ⟩freezing4 + 1 64π2 (Σh + ΣV -ΣF total ) = 3 4πL2 		(18)

0.4.2 Scenario B: Effective Self-Interaction in the Neutrino Condensate Model

The alternative scenario of neutrino Cooper pairs completely abandons the introduction of new scalar fields. At the IR boundary of the Universe (μ = 1∕L), neutrinos cease to behave as free QFT fermions. Due to weak gravitational attraction, three generations of ultrarelativistic neutrinos combine into macroscopic Cooper pairs, forming a coherent Bose-condensate (the mechanism [3]) with the equation of state of a liquid vacuum (P = -ρ). Mechanism of sign inversion: The transition of the neutrino sector into a BCS-superfluid state fundamentally changes its thermodynamic status. Instead of fermionic zero-point oscillations, a collective bosonic macrostate arises — a condensate of Cooper pairs, the energy sign of which is strictly positive. The effective potential of the Bose-condensate ρ⟨νν⟩ is revealed through the effective four-fermion self-interaction constant Gνν and the magnitude of the phase energy gap Δν:

ρνeffective ≡ ρ ⟨νν⟩ = +1 2GννΔν4 > 0 		(19)

The equation of the total constraint-free vacuum budget in this representation closes with allowance for the UV/IR black hole boundaries [5]:

1 2GννΔν4 + 1 64π2 (Σh + ΣV -ΣSM\νtotal ) = 3 4πL2 		(20)

0.4.3 Derivation of the Fundamental Neutrino Mass from the Residual Lambda Value

In both emergent scenarios, the physical scale forming the positive cosmological residue ~ +10-123 after the mutual subtraction of the macroscopic contributions of the top quark and self-interaction is rigidly locked to the neutrino mass through the phase energy gap (Δν ~ mν). The energy density of the zero-point oscillations of three generations of neutrinos is given by the canonical expression of the effective potential [1]:

Σneutrinos = 2 ∑ j∈e,μ,τmνj4 (ln ⁡ (m νj2L2) -3 2 ) 		(21)

Since the logarithmic term at the horizon scale is stable ( ln ⁡ (mν2L2) ~ 195), and the residual vacuum background must strictly account for the neutrino mass, we equate the total vacuum energy density of the Universe (the Schwarzschild black hole formula) to the effective positive neutrino contribution, following the UV/IR profile of the effective action [5]:

3 4πL2 ≈ 1 64π2 ⋅ 6mν4 ⋅ ln ⁡ (m ν2L2) 		(22)

where mν is the geometric mean value of the masses of the three neutrino generations in Planck units. Substituting the invariant horizon scale L ≈ 1061 and solving the equation for the mass, we find the exact algebraic dependence on the IR radius of the Universe L:

mν ~ L-1∕2 = (1061)-1∕2 = 10-30.5 		(23)

Converting the obtained dimensionless value from the Planck system to standard physical units of electronvolts (1 MPl ≈ 1.22 × 1028 eV), we obtain the exact IR mass:

mν ~ 10-30.5 × (1.22 × 1028 eV) ≈ 3.8 × 10-3 eV ≈ 0.004 eV 		(24)

The derived value of the average neutrino mass of the order of 10-3 eV (0.004 eV) is in ideal agreement with modern IR constraints from oscillation experiments (confirming the mass squared difference Δm2 ~ 10-5 - 10-3 eV2).

0.5 Main Conclusions

1.
UV/IR Isomorphism of the de Sitter Black Hole [5]: The quantum field energy balance of the vacuum of the Universe is not arbitrary, but is completely dictated and bounded by the classical Schwarzschild formula for closed systems (M0 = L). This connection is strictly satisfied for the de Sitter Universe and determines the fundamental IR/UV interaction (infrared-ultraviolet connection) between the local quantum vacuum energy density (UV) and the maximum size of the cosmological event horizon (IR) [5]. Apparently, it is precisely the Standard Model with its gauge and chiral symmetries SU(3)C × SU(2)L × U(1)Y that generates such a strictly determined mass spectrum of elementary particles that ensures the exact IR-UV interconnection. This fact allows completely excluding the geometric constant Λgeom from the action Lagrangian, reducing the macroscopic elasticity of the vacuum to collective quantum dynamics.
2.
Experimental Gate of the Large Hadron Collider: The mathematical structure of the obtained QFT balance gives a direct and rigorous explanation for the negative results of searches for New Physics at CERN. No supersymmetric partners or teraelectronvolt fields were discovered at the LHC accelerator, because the real vacuum components of the Universe are fully equipped and closed exclusively by the fields of the Standard Model. Quantum modes of matter must physically be born directly from the vacuum, and the addition of any heavy sectors would violate the precision UV/IR compensation of the top quark, destroying the stability of the horizon L.
3.
Fermionic Sign Inversion: Within the framework of emergent space-time regeneration, the neutrino acts as the main cosmological regulator. Due to collective effects (Wetterich’s cosmon coupling [4] or the Cooper Bose-condensate [3]), the neutrino sector completely inverts its QFT sign to positive, generating the observed Dark Energy density. The neutrino mass of the order of 0.004 eV derived from first principles serves as a direct, experimentally verified testament to the closed nature of the Universe on the lightest fermionic sector of the SM.

Bibliography

[1]   Coleman, S., and Weinberg, E. (1973). Radiative Corrections as the Origin of Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking. Physical Review D, 7(6), 1888-1910.

[2]   Bardeen, W. A., Buras, A. J., Duke, D. W., and Muta, T. (1978). Deep-inelastic scattering beyond the leading order in asymptotically free gauge theories. Physical Review D, 18(11), 3998.

[3]   Jitesh R. Bhatt, Utpal Sarkar, “Majorana Neutrino Superfluidity and Stability of Neutrino Dark Energy,” Phys. Rev. D,80, p. 045016,2009

[4]   Wetterich, C. (1993). Exact evolution equation for the effective potential. Physics Letters B, 301(1), 90-94.

[5]   Cohen, A. G., Kaplan, D. B., and Nelson, A. E. (1999). Effective Field Theory, Black Holes, and the Cosmological Constant. Physical Review Letters, 82(25), 4971-4974 (Preprint arXiv:hep-th/9803132, 1998).

Part XIV
Part 14. Macroscopic Mechanisms of Coherent Structures: Dynamical Caverns and GW-Geons as the Thermodynamic Foundation of the Dark Sector

Abstract. Macroscopic mechanisms of spatial metric destabilization of isolated masses on a dynamic cosmological background and ways to resolve this impasse through the concept of regular cores with a vacuum equation of state are considered. An alternative scenario for energy redistribution between baryonic matter and the vacuum is investigated, where spacetime torsion, generated by the spin density of the fermionic substrate, acts as a bypass mechanism. The process of system self-regulation is described, in which a decrease in matter density passively disables the torsional exchange and stabilizes the density ratio of the components. Additionally, the mechanism of excess mass generation in the early Universe due to the formation of coherent wave geometries functioning as spherical bulk resonators for gravitational radiation is studied. The stability of such macrostructures is substantiated by the balance between the non-linear self-attraction of the field and the dynamic injection of kinetic energy from the rotating core, fixed by higher quantum corrections in curvature. It is established that the spatial configuration of trapped radiation is perceived by a macroscopic observer as a halo of hidden mass, which eliminates the need to introduce hypothetical particles. The final bifurcation decay channels of coherent systems are described, leading to the complete dissipation of the hidden mass into the background Bose-condensate of vacuum energy and ensuring global information conservation.

0.1 Topological Destruction of the McVittie Metric, Genesis of DESI GEODs, and Phase Decay of Regular Black Holes

Historically, the first attempt at an analytical description of an isolated static mass immersed in a dynamic cosmological Friedmann–Lemaître–Robertson–Walker (FLRW) background is the McVittie metric [1]. In comoving spatial coordinates, its interval is written in the form:

ds2 = - (1 -M 2r ) 2 (1 + M 2r ) 2dt2 + a2(t) (1 + M 2r )4(dr2 + r2dΩ2) 		(1)

where M = const is the local mass parameter, and a(t) is the scale factor of the cosmological background.

An analysis of this geometry reveals two critical scales at which the metric fabric of GR undergoes destabilization. The local destruction scale is fixed at the comoving surface r = M∕2, where an explicit dynamic divergence of the fluid pressure is discovered during the calculation of the Ricci scalar R [2]:

lim ⁡ r→M 2 R(t,r) = lim ⁡ r→M 2 [6 (ä a + ȧ2 a2 ) 1 + M 2r (1 -M 2r ) 3 ] →∞ 		(2)

provided that ȧ(t)≠0. This limiting transition proves that for any non-stationary Universe, the surface of the object’s local horizon turns into a spacelike curvature singularity.

The global behavior of the structure is determined through the transition to the areal coordinate (the physical radius of the area of the isolating 2-sphere) R(t,r) = a(t)r(1 + M∕2r)2. The kinematic condition for the "trapping" of radial light rays, which determines the coordinate position of the apparent horizon of the object (R1 ≈ 2M) and the cosmological Hubble horizon (R2 ≈ 1∕H), is given by the vanishing of the total derivative of the area radius along the light path (dR∕dt = 0). Substituting this geometric condition into the GR field equations (Einstein equations) for the McVittie metric, the system reduces to the cubic equation H2R3 - R + 2M = 0.

From the analysis of the discriminant of this polynomial, it strictly follows that if the compatibility criterion is violated:

M ⋅ H(t) ≤ 1 33 		(3)

the roots of the equation become complex, and the McVittie metric has no physical (real) solution in general relativity [1]. This makes holding a static mass parameter M = const on a dynamic FLRW background fundamentally impossible.

Within the framework of the concept of non-singular configurations of the general Gliner–Bardeen–Dymnikova class [3,4], as well as their quantum-informational generalizations in the form of Brustein’s "frozen stars" [5,6], this geometric impasse is resolved by a macroscopic phase transition. The central singularity is eliminated, and the internal metric is reorganized into a regular core filled with a vacuum condensate with a de Sitter equation of state for Dark Energy (P = -ρ).

As an alternative secondary scenario, the concept of GEODs (Generic Objects of Dark Energy) by the DESI collaboration (the Crocker–Weiner model) is considered in modern literature [7,8], where due to the continuous work of Hubble expansion over the internal anisotropic pressure, the object’s mass growth is phenomenologically scaled as M(t) ∝ a3(t).

However, regardless of the specific internal parametrization, exceeding the limit MH > 1 33 triggers an inevitable topological relaxation of the system. Due to the thermodynamic disadvantage of the ordered internal state of the vacuum core under conditions of continuous elastic tension of the expanding space, the system reaches its metastability limit. At a critical value of the external scale factor, a spontaneous destruction of the local apparent horizon occurs. At this moment, the localized metric defect is neutralized, and all Dark Energy accumulated inside is radially emitted into the external space. This high-intensity burst completely dissipates across the Anti-PG coordinate grid, relaxing into the global, homogeneous background Bose-condensate of the Universe’s Dark Energy, which ensures the maximization of the total entropy of the system in accordance with the second law of thermodynamics.

Thus, within the framework of the classical Friedmann–Robertson–Walker (FRW) cosmological background filled with standard matter or radiation, the existence of a regular, non-accreting black hole is impossible due to the inevitable emergence of pressure singularities on its horizon. However, there is a fundamental exception to this rule, strictly proven in the work of Kaloper, Kleban, and Martin [11]. Studying the class of solutions to Einstein’s equations once proposed by McVittie, the authors showed that if the evolution of the cosmos at late stages begins to be dominated by a positive cosmological constant (Λ > 0), the physical situation changes radically. The exponential expansion of space in the de Sitter phase dilutes the surrounding FRW fluid near the gravitational radius so rapidly that all pressure inhomogeneities and higher curvature invariants tend to zero. In the future limit (t →∞), a strictly stable, regular, and anomaly-free solution — the Schwarzschild–de Sitter geometry — forms at the site of the spacetime defect. Thus, a black hole is capable of harmoniously coexisting with the cosmological background only when the Universe completes its evolution and itself asymptotically transforms into a pure de Sitter vacuum.

0.2 Geometric Scenario of Energy Redistribution Between Baryonic Matter and the Vacuum

Along with hydrodynamic models, there is an alternative, strictly geometric scenario for energy redistribution between baryonic matter and the vacuum, implemented within Einstein–Cartan gravity. Unlike general relativity, where the connection of space is symmetric, in the Einstein–Cartan theory the affine connection tensor Γμνρ contains an antisymmetric part with respect to the lower indices — the torsion tensor Sμνρ = Γ [μν]ρ. According to Cartan’s equations, the source of this torsion at the macroscopic level is the averaged internal spin density of fermions (baryonic matter). Within the framework of the cosmological principle for a homogeneous and isotropic medium, this spin contribution collapses into a single scalar function of time Φ(t) ∝ a-3 (where a is the scale factor of the Universe), which has the dimensionality of frequency (s-1) and characterizes the intensity of space torsion [13].

The presence of torsion deforms the geometry of spacetime, which directly changes the classical covariant conservation law for the energy-momentum tensor of the medium. If we compare the modified Friedmann equations with the hydrodynamic model of two media, the effective dimensionless dark energy pressure parameter ωX, defined as:

ωX ≡ pX ρX 		(4)

where pX is the volumetric pressure and ρX is the energy density of the vacuum sector, acquires a dynamic addition of the form [12]:

ωX = ωm -r + 1 r 1 3H [2H(2 - q) (Φ H ) 2 1 + (Φ H ) 2 ] 		(5)

In equation (2), the following macroscopic variables are used:

  • ωm ≡ pm∕ρm — the dimensionless pressure coefficient of baryonic matter (pm and ρm are its pressure and energy density, respectively; for a non-relativistic gas ωm ≈ 0).

  • H ≡ȧ∕a — the Hubble parameter (s-1), setting the local expansion rate of the spatial manifold.

  • q ≡-aä∕ȧ2 — the dimensionless deceleration parameter, which determines the rate of change of the spatial expansion velocity.

  • r ≡ ρm∕ρX — the instantaneous dimensionless ratio of the volumetric energy densities of baryons and the vacuum.

The physical meaning of equation (2) becomes transparent if we compare the expression in square brackets with classical phenomenological models of energy exchange between interacting dark sectors [14]. In such models, to describe the irreversible transfer of energy from one sector to another, a dissipative source Q is phenomenologically introduced into the right-hand sides of the continuity equations [14]:

ρ˙m + 3H(ρm + pm) = Q 		(6)

ρ˙X + 3H(ρX + pX) = -Q 		(7)

When choosing a linear source of the form Q = -Γρm, where Γ (s-1) represents the total decay (transfer) rate of matter, the joint solution of equations (3) and (4) leads to a dynamic change in the effective pressure. It must be emphasized that the very concept according to which a decreasing baryonic mass (or matter density) acts as a dynamic donor for the dark energy sector has deep roots in theoretical cosmology. The idea of non-conservative dark sectors goes back to the pioneering works of the late 1990s and early 2000s, which formulated the paradigm of interacting/coupled dark energy [14]. Within these classical models, the phenomenological energy exchange Q was introduced to overcome the cosmic coincidence problem and was described through a direct Lagrangian interaction of baryons (or dark matter) with the scalar field of quintessence [14]. However, a fundamental limitation of approaches like "coupled quintessence" has always been the need to introduce hypothetical scalar fields and artificial coupling constants, the nature of which finds no direct confirmation in elementary particle physics. In this context, the recent results of Yun, Kim, and Lee [12] represent a qualitative step forward, placing Amendola’s old phenomenological idea on the rigorous tracks of macroscopic geometry. In the model [12], artificial scalar fields are completely abolished, and the role of the bypass valve (pump) transitions to the torsion of spacetime Φ(t), which, according to the Einstein–Cartan equations, is unavoidably generated by the spin density of the really existing fermionic substrate of the Universe [13]. Thus, the dissipative term Q, previously introduced heuristically "by hand" into the balance equations [14], turns out in the modern torsion interpretation to be a natural kinematic consequence of the geometric structure of spacetime with torsion. From a comparison of the systems, it strictly follows that the macroscopic space torsion Φ(t) in equations (2) acts equivalently to the dissipative source Q, imposing on the system an effective rate of interdisciplinary energy exchange:

Γeff = 2H(2 - q) (Φ H ) 2 1 + (Φ H ) 2 		(8)

Since the function Φ(t) is generated by baryon spins, the Einstein–Cartan geometry realizes a self-regulation regime for the system. While the matter density is high, the dimensionless torsion parameter (Φ∕H)2 is large, which reduces the vacuum pressure parameter ωX to deeply negative values (ωX < -1∕3), triggering accelerated spatial expansion. As matter undergoes dilution, the torsion "pump" passively turns off (Φ → 0), which naturally stops the energy flow and stabilizes the dimensionless density ratio at the stationary value r0 ≡ r(t0) ≈ 0.429 observed in the current cosmological epoch t0 [12].

0.3 Quasi-Stationary Wheeler–Misner GW-Geons Around Red Dots and the Papapetrou–Reissner Shutter

The main macroscopic mechanism for generating excess masses in the early Universe (JWST Little Red Dots) is the formation of dynamic GW-geons in the Anti-Painlev’e–Gullstrand geometry. When the dimensionless spin of a baryonic rotator is over-spun above the Kerr barrier (a* = 1 + ϵ), the triaxial Jacobi–Darwin ellipsoid begins to generate continuous quadrupole gravitational radiation with a power of LGW ~ 1046 erg/s. In a strong non-linear field, the energy-momentum density of the gravitational radiation itself, Tij(GW), contributes to the total energy-momentum tensor of the system, inducing its own gravitational attraction of fields [2]. A coherent macrostructure forms around the central source — the Wheeler–Misner GW-geon [9,10], functioning as a spherical three-dimensional resonator in which gravitational radiation is localized as a standing wave. The stability of this dynamic system is ensured by the balance of two independent mechanisms:

1.
Gravitational self-attraction of the field (sector Tij): Non-linear terms of Einstein’s equations hold back the front of gravitational waves, preventing their free scattering to infinity.
2.
Dynamic energy injection (the Papapetrou–Reissner method): The continuous transfer of kinetic energy from the rotating core is carried out through the non-diagonal components of the energy-momentum tensor T0i. This dynamic momentum (the frame-dragging effect) compensates for the dissipative losses of the geon, preventing the radial collapse of the structure.

A classical geon in pure general relativity is metastable. Final stabilization of the structure over macroscopic times τ ~ 283 Myr is realized due to the inclusion of higher non-linear quantum gravity corrections in curvature R2. The quantum backpressure of vacuum stiffness across the macroscopic gap Δr = M(a*2 - 1) forms an effective potential barrier — a non-linear GW-EM shutter that fixes the geometric scales of the geon.

0.4 The GW-Geon as the Material Basis of Dark Matter in the Early Universe and the Bifurcation Finale of the System

The coherent GW-geon formed around the Red Dot possesses a significant effective mass-energy distributed throughout the volume of the galactic cavern. This spatial configuration of an extended gravitating mass, rigidly bound to the central baryonic core through the interference coupling of the mean-field wave functions Ψfull (according to Part 6), is interpreted by a macroscopic observer as a galactic Dark Matter halo. Thus, the phenomenon of Dark Matter is generated not by hypothetical fundamental particles, but by a macroscopic halo of coherent gravitational radiation trapped in a non-linear vacuum resonator. The dynamic evolution and phase finale of the system are determined by the depletion time of the kinetic energy of the central baryonic rotator. When the injection term zeroes out (T0i → 0), the GW-geon loses coherence, and the system undergoes bifurcation into two channels depending on the critical energy density of the gravitational hum:

1.
Gravitational collapse channel: At supercritical energy density, the field self-attraction in the Tij(GW) sector dominates, causing a rapid collapse of the standing wave into matterless black holes. By virtue of the metastability of classical horizons on an expanding background proven in Section 1, these intermediate objects eventually exit the GR stability limit, undergo topological relaxation, and explosively emit their entire mass-energy into the external space as Dark Energy.
2.
Direct dissipation channel: At subcritical energy density, the elastic self-attraction of the waves is insufficient to hold the soliton. The standing wave of GW radiation experiences direct phase relaxation: high-frequency modes dissipate into the thermal electromagnetic spectrum, and the long-wavelength gravitational invariant is uniformly distributed across the flat Anti-PG coordinate grid.

In both evolutionary scenarios, the final product of decay is the complete transition of the local Dark Matter halo energy into the global Dark Energy Bose-condensate (Λeff), increasing the total entropy of the Universe to S0 ≈ 10122 bits on the external holographic screen, which perfectly closes the general law of information conservation of the trilogy.

Part XV
Part 15. Non-equilibrium Thermodynamics of Open GR Systems: Unitary Limit, Prigogine’s Dissipative Structures, and DM → DE Informational Transit

Abstract. The evolution of open systems within the framework of non-equilibrium thermodynamics and the principle of preservation of unitarity inside a fixed cosmological horizon is considered. On the basis of covariant conservation laws, a continuous information transit is described, in which the evacuation of baryonic matter beyond the causal boundary is compensated by the emergent reorganization of the hidden sector. It is established that the character of vacuum structure formation is determined by the degree of the system’s distance from thermodynamic equilibrium. In the strongly non-equilibrium regime of the early Universe, the maximization of the entropy growth rate leads to the spontaneous crystallization of coherent spatial defects and torsional configurations performing the macroscopic function of ordered heat and mass transfer cells. The subsequent transition of the system to a weakly non-equilibrium regime inducing the generation of low-energy dissipative structures at the molecular level of biological cells, whose spatial scales are rigidly fixed by the holographic limit of ultraviolet cutoff, is described. A mechanism of resonant coupling of the frequency parameters of cellular organelles with the frequency profile of the hidden mass, providing a continuous shedding of intra-systemic entropic chaos into the vacuum thermostat, is revealed. The concept of interstellar communication based on the modulation of macroscopic quantum states in resonance with the spectrum of the hidden sector is developed. Scenarios of infrared freezing of long-range fluctuations of additional scalar fields in strong gravitational fields are analyzed. It is shown that the acceleration of the background macrogeometry of planetary systems suppresses the coupling constant of the scalar sector, seamlessly restoring the equations of the linear theory of gravity. Meanwhile, the algebraic torsion communication channel, rigidly tied to the local spin density of the medium, remains active on galactic scales, predetermining the dynamics of the hidden mass on the periphery of systems.

0.1 Strongly Non-equilibrium Cosmological Regime: Gyarmati’s Variational Principle and Cosmic Bénard Cells

For a local macroscopic observer, our Universe is located inside a sphere whose size is rigidly bounded by a limiting (maximum) event horizon. Within this fixed volume, the evolution of the quantum states of the Universe strictly obeys the principle of unitarity, which requires the absolute constancy of its total effective mass-energy and the preservation of all Noether information.

However, due to the cosmological vacuum shift in the Anti-Painlevé–Gullstrand metric, baryonic matter and high-frequency radiation are continuously evacuated (fly away) beyond this limiting event horizon. To prevent a non-unitary loss of quantum states and compensate for the local mass deficit, the covariant conservation law of the full energy-momentum tensor and the Bianchi identities predetermine an automatic rearrangement of the local geometry. The baryonic matter and light flying beyond the physical boundary are replaced by an equivalent informational contribution from the Dark Sector — Dark Matter (DM) and Dark Energy (DE).

In this strongly coupled correlated system, Dark Matter acts as a fundamental quantum-informational shutter. It is necessary to preserve unitarity and functions as a physical carrier that holds and preserves information about non-local correlations of the baryons remaining inside the horizon with the baryons that have flown beyond the event horizon boundaries. Thus, a constant pumping of information occurs from the open baryonic component into the hidden information of Dark Energy through Dark Matter.

The dynamics of this information transit and entropy generation obeys the laws of non-equilibrium thermodynamics of open systems. According to Ilya Prigogine’s theory [2], the character of the system’s evolution is rigidly divided depending on the degree of its distance from thermodynamic equilibrium:

  • Weakly non-equilibrium regime (Late Universe): The system obeys Prigogine’s classical principle of the minimum of entropy production [2]. The rate of chaos generation is minimal, and the DM → DE transition proceeds through a smooth, quasi-static relaxation of fields without macroscopic structural jumps.

  • Strongly non-equilibrium regime (Early Universe, epoch of Red Dots): The system is at an extreme distance from equilibrium. In this phase, the local linear law is violated, and the thermodynamic principle of maximization of the entropy growth rate comes into force, described by István Gyarmati’s variational principle [3]. Entropy growth in the vacuum is maximized, and ordered dissipative structures performing this growth spontaneously arise in it.

Just as in classical hydrodynamics a critical thermal gradient generates ordered convective Bénard cells, a highly organized dissipative structure spontaneously crystallizes in the strongly non-equilibrium gravitational field of the early Universe [4]. The elements of this single Prigogine dissipative structure of the early Universe are the macroscopic GR anomalies considered in detail in Part 14: compact rotators (JWST Red Dots), Wheeler–Misner quasi-stationary GW-geons [5], Brustein–Dymnikova regular black holes [6,7], cosmological GEODs [8], and Cartan torsion [14,15].

Their coherent and coordinated behavior on the macroscale is explained by the high correlation of the dissipative structure itself, which links spatially separated metric anomalies through fluctuations of a single quantum field of the vacuum. Acting as ordered cosmic "Bénard cells," these strongly correlated structures maximize entropy growth and provide an avalanche-like transition of baryonic information into the hidden energy of the vacuum.

0.2 Small-Scale Low-Energy Bénard Cells: Quantum Biological Coherence, Global Solaris Ocean, and DM-Mediated SETI 2.0 Networks

At late stages of cosmological evolution, as the Universe transitions into a weakly non-equilibrium regime, the macroscopic synthesis of ultra-high-energy GR structures ceases. However, the universal thermodynamic law [2] initiates the appearance of a new kind of dissipative structures — significantly less energy-intensive, but possessing an extremely high information packaging density. This new kind of Prigogine "Bénard cells" at late stages becomes living matter on the scale of a biological cell.

The fundamental spatial scale of an elementary living unit is strictly predetermined by the geometric mean between the global radius of the maximum event horizon of the Universe Rh and the microscopic Planck quantum limit ℓPl:

Lcell ~Rh ⋅ ℓPl ≈ 40 ÷ 50μm 		(1)

This is the holographic CKN bound [1]. Indeed, according to the condition of Cohen, Kaplan, and Nelson, an effective quantum field theory in a volume of radius Rh remains applicable and does not lead to collapse into a black hole as long as the total energy (or mass) of the system does not exceed the black hole mass of the same radius: E ≤ MBH ~Rh G ~ Rh Lp2, where Lp = Gℏ∕c3 is the Planck length (in natural units ℏ = c = 1).

If we cut off the spectrum of modes in a resonator of radius Rh at some maximum wave number kmax (corresponding to the maximum frequency ωmax), then the total number of quantum modes N in this volume V = 4 3πRh3 is standardly estimated as: N ~ V ⋅ kmax3 ~ R h3k max3. For each mode with a wave number k, the zero-point oscillation energy is E0 = 1 2ℏω. For relativistic particles (or photons), the dispersion law is linear: ω = ck (in natural units ℏ = c = 1, we get ω = k).

To find the total vacuum energy density ρ, we integrate the energies of these zero-point oscillations over the entire available phase space (momenta) from zero to the cutoff momentum kmax:

ρ = ∫ 0kmax d3k (2π)3 (1 2ω) = 1 2(2π)3 ∫ 0kmax 4πk2 ⋅ kdk ~ k max4 		(2)

Since the energy of each individual mode grows linearly with momentum ( ~ k), and the density of the modes themselves in space grows as the volume of a sphere in momentum space ( ~ k3), their product during integration yields the fourth power of the upper cutoff limit.

Let us substitute the expression for the total energy into the CKN bound: Rh3k max4 ~ Rh Lp2⟹kmax4 ~ 1 Rh2Lp2. Extracting the fourth root, we obtain the required boundary mode (cutoff momentum): kmax ~ 1 Rh Lp. Consequently, the wavelength of the minimum mode (ultraviolet cutoff) must be proportional to the geometric mean between the resonator size and the Planck length: λmin ~Rh Lp.

This geometric invariant fixes the size of a living cell as the optimal spatial gap in which the molecular structure is capable of maintaining a stable connection with the macrogenic Universe. The interaction of living matter with the global Universe occurs through Dark Matter. This interaction is possible exclusively for systems highly ordered at the quantum level, unlike ordinary macroscopic matter, which strictly obeys classical Newtonian interaction. The nature of quantum macrostates in biological objects is revised relative to the classical Orch OR hypothesis of Penrose and Hameroff [10]. In our model, objective reduction (collapse) of the wave function at this level is completely excluded, as it would violate the unitarity of the closed volume inside the event horizon. The characteristic time equation of Di’osi and Penrose [9], calculated through the values of the interacting masses, spatial distances between them, and the geometric form factor:

τP = ℏ ΔEG = ℏ G ⋅ d m2 ⋅ f (R d ) 		(3)

replaces the moment of reduction. The Penrose time τP at the level of the cell’s cytoskeletal microtubules sets the intrinsic frequency spectrum of Dark Matter (ωTM ~ 1∕τP). When the internal frequencies of microtubules coincide with the frequency profile of the DM halo, what occurs at times τP is not a collapse of states, but an ordered quantum entanglement of the biological structure with Dark Matter and a continuous transfer of entropic information (intra-systemic chaos) into Dark Energy via the Yun–Kim–Lee mechanism on higher harmonics of the torsion field [14,15]. Dark Energy in this scheme plays the role of a gigantic thermal reservoir. This global chaotic thermostat of DE is completely analogous to the thinking Solaris Ocean in the novel of the same name by Stanislaw Lem [11]. It represents a non-local informational matrix of the Universe, which accumulates the transferred entropic chaos of living systems through the DM halo and holds the memory of each entangled baryon in its quantum correlations. Within the framework of the Agency of Life concept by Angus McCoss [12] and the multi-agent infodynamic approach MEGAM [13], this consensus of Life forms distributed spheres of correlation (observer halos) in the habitable zones of galaxies. The conclusion about DM-mediated communication reshapes the basic paradigm of the search for extraterrestrial intelligence (SETI). If highly developed extraterrestrial civilizations exist in the Universe, then a logically inevitable milestone of their evolution is the abandonment of light and radio signals (due to energy dissipation proportional to 1∕r2) and the transition to communication through Dark Matter. Interstellar communication is implemented via DM communication networks (SETI 2.0). Relay nodes of SETI 2.0 are placed in regions where the influence of Dark Matter is maximal: at the gravitational Lagrange points of stellar systems and on the peripheral edges of galactic disks, at the boundaries of DM halos. Transmission of such signals is possible through coherent oscillatory relaxation of artificially ordered quantum macrostates of the transmitter that are in exact resonance with the frequency spectrum of DM. Modulation of quantum entanglement instantaneously rearranges the density matrix of the Universe. Reception of SETI 2.0 signals is realized by measuring the dynamic shifts of the instantaneous phases themselves, as well as the mutual cross-correlations of phase noises of remote quantum frequency standards (atomic clocks). The passage of a non-local DM pulse through a spatial array of atomic clocks is recorded as a synchronous phase anomaly highlighted by methods of correlation analysis of atomic matrix noises without violating the Noether mass balance of the Universe.

0.3 Dark Matter and Dark Energy in the Late Universe

The concept of the cosmon scalar field as a dynamic source of dark energy (quintessence) underwent a complex multi-stage evolution, during which views on the cosmic coincidence problem and mechanisms of quantum corrections changed radically.

0.3.1 The Coincidence Problem ("Why Now?") and Quantum Corrections

In Christof Wetterich’s original model (1988), the cosmon was treated as a classical scalar field of quintessence that rolled smoothly down a gentle potential [17,18]. The main problem of this early paradigm was the need for fine manual tuning of the mathematical potential so that the moment of "turning on" the accelerated expansion of the Universe accidentally coincided with our astronomical epoch. The second fundamental vulnerability lay in the complete ignorance of the virtual particles of the Standard Model. Any attempt to account for radiative corrections and add the contribution of the top quark or the Higgs boson to the cosmon potential equations led to a catastrophic explosion of quantum vacuum energy, flying up 56 orders of magnitude relative to observed cosmological parameters. In the modern quantum field balance, this physics has finally united. The functional renormalization group method (the continuous Wetterich flow) demonstrates that the cosmon no longer exists "by itself" in isolation from elementary particle physics [17]. It is mathematically obligated to compensate for the top quark: the positive self-interaction potential of the cosmon (1 4λχ4) represents not a random constant, but an exact dynamic counterweight to the fermionic pressure of the Standard Model vacuum matter.

0.3.2 Development of Models with Variable Neutrino Mass

In parallel, a strong theoretical direction formed in cosmology linking the dark energy density with the properties of the relict neutrino background. The development of the neutrino dark energy (DE) concept followed the path of complicating the structure of the relict neutrino background (RNB) — from a structureless gas to a macroscopic quantum condensate.

(1. Classical MaVaNs models and the instability problem (2003–2005):) In the original MaVaNs (Mass Varying Neutrinos) models of Kaplan, Nelson, Fardon, and Weiner [21,22], neutrinos were considered as a free Fermi gas. Their mass dynamically depended on the density of an external scalar field — the acceleron. In 2005, Afshordi, Zaldarriaga, and Kohri [23] proved that the exchange of quanta of this field generates an excess long-range attraction between fermions. This led to an imaginary speed of sound (cs2 < 0) and a catastrophic hydrodynamic instability: the homogeneous DE medium fragmented instantly into dense local clusters (neutrino nuggets).

(2. Scalar Cooper pairing (2008):) To stabilize MaVaNs, Bhatt and Sarkar [24] applied the apparatus of the Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer (BCS) theory to cosmological media for the first time. They showed that during the cooling of the expanding Universe, the strong attraction through the acceleron field inevitably combines Majorana or Dirac neutrinos into Cooper pairs. The resulting macroscopic superfluid condensate creates a positive quantum pressure that compensates for the scalar attraction, stops the background collapse, and keeps the DE equation of state stable.

(3. Gauge pairing without scalar fields (2007–2009):) In parallel, alternative models developed where the authors abandoned the hypothetical acceleron and declared the binding energy of the neutrino condensate itself as the source of DE:

  • Electroweak mechanism (de Vega, 2007): Within de Vega’s model [26], it was shown that in the curved Friedmann–Robertson–Walker spacetime at ultra-low temperatures, the standard weak interaction (exchange of Standard Model gauge Z-bosons) is enhanced. This causes vacuum instability and spontaneous generation of Cooper pairs of left-handed neutrinos, whose energy density is equivalent to the cosmological constant.

  • Electromagnetic mechanism (Horvat, Minkowski, Trampetic, 2009): In this scheme [25], pairing is induced by the anomalous magnetic dipole (or anapole) moment of massive neutrinos. When the RNB temperature drops, long-range magnetic forces between dipoles begin to dominate over kinetic energy, binding neutrinos with antiparallel spins into singlet pairs and transforming the background equation of state. Thus, the idea of Cooper pairs evolved from an external mechanism for stabilizing scalar fields (Bhatt, Sarkar) to a self-sufficient source of dark energy due to internal interactions of the Standard Model (de Vega, Horvat).

0.3.3 Three Steps of the Evolution of Wetterich’s Theory

In an attempt to stitch these paradigms together, Christof Wetterich successively put forward three fundamentally different physical models:

(Step 1. The "Cosmon Dark Matter" model (2001–2002):) Wetterich considered a pure cosmon field without the participation of the neutrino background [17,18]. Initially, the DE field was ideally smooth and even throughout the Universe. However, short-wavelength quantum fluctuations existed in it. The mathematics of the potential was written in such a way that these short waves began to grow non-linearly, fragment, and gather into stable gravitational lumps. The smooth part of the field remained Dark Energy, and these heavier lumps of the cosmon itself began to behave as heavy non-relativistic particles, forming Dark Matter.

(Step 2. The "Neutrino clustering" model (2007–2009):) Realizing the potential of neutrino coupling, Wetterich rewrote the equations [19]. The cosmon rolled freely while the Universe expanded. But at the moment when the relict neutrinos cooled and turned from relativistic particles into ordinary non-relativistic matter, their masses began to dynamically grow. This "elastic" resistance of the neutrino sector physically stopped (froze) the cosmon scalar field. Its stoppage automatically turned on negative pressure and launched DE. The neutrinos worked as a cosmic "alarm clock" that turned on dark energy precisely in our epoch. At the same time, the coupling of the cosmon and neutrinos gave rise to a new "fifth force," which is many times stronger than gravity. Under its action, neutrino fermions began an avalanche-like compression into huge non-linear clusters.

(Step 3. The "Neutrino lump fluid" model (2013):) Together with Motta and Pettorino, Wetterich developed this idea into a full concept of a neutrino lumpy fluid (Neutrino lump fluid) [20]. Here, the cosmon-neutrino "fifth force" forcibly pulls cosmic relict neutrinos toward each other on the scales of galaxy superclusters, forming massive structures — neutrino lumps. By themselves, these fermionic lumps are not the classical DM of galaxies, but due to their colossal mass, they gravitationally drag Cold Dark Matter (CDM) along, forcing it to cluster faster. On cosmological scales, this entire bubbling fluid system dynamically mimics the behavior of Cold DM.

As a synthesis of these steps, a hypothesis is put forward that eliminates the impasse of classical fermionic clouds: the cosmon acts as a quantum mediator that combines neutrinos into Cooper pairs, transitioning the system from a fermionic status to a macroscopic Bose-condensate whose lumps form DM.

0.4 Torsion Scalar of the Universe and the Weyssenhoff Fluid

The specified dark matter condensate naturally forms torsion in the spacetime geometry of Einstein–Cartan [27–29]. Modern cosmological models of Yun, Kim, and Lee [14,15] develop these ideas by considering the generation of the spin current of a Weyssenhoff fermion fluid [16], which includes both the fermions of baryonic matter and the Bose-condensate of dark matter. The authors introduced a dimensionless invariant of torsional deformation determined by the current balance of energy densities:

( Φ H )2 = Ω m + d2 - 1 ≈ 0.36 		(4)

where Ωm is the dimensionless matter density, and d is the holographic parameter of effective field theory (IR cutoff at the Hubble radius). From this relation, it follows that the average cosmological torsion scalar of the Universe is extremely small:

Φ0 ~ 10-19s-1 		(5)

which is due to the low average density of the intergalactic fermionic gas of baryons and dark matter [14]. This frequency is fundamentally close to the natural frequency of a free cosmon, corresponding to its ultra-light rest mass in a smooth state (mc ~ 10-33 eV), at which the de Broglie wavelength is commensurate with the radius of the observable Universe. However, torsion in Einstein–Cartan gravity is a local algebraic field rigidly tied to the local spin density of the medium [16]. The generation of the spin current of the Weyssenhoff fluid in dark matter lumps locally increases the spin density by 28 orders of magnitude. This leads to a dynamic weighting of the effective field mass inside galaxy halos (where the de Broglie wavelength shrinks to astronomical scales) and drives the local torsion frequency up to the gigahertz scale:

Φloc ≈tens of GHz 		(6)

0.5 Phenomenological Implications and IR Effects

It is assumed that the torsion or tension of dark matter at the edges of galaxies and at the Lagrange points can directly interact with baryonic matter through two mechanisms: through direct spin interaction with fermions and through local torsional deformation of spacetime. These interactions open up a physical explanation for a number of observed anomalies:

1.
Astrophysical anomalies: Anomalous non-gravitational accelerations of spacecraft at the boundaries of systems (the Voyager effect).
2.
‘Oumuamua phenomenon: The anomalous acceleration of the interstellar asteroid 1I/‘Oumuamua is interpreted as the behavior of a macroscopic cluster of highly ordered spins (a natural spin condensate) coherently interacting with the local gigahertz torsion of DM ("ghost sail effect").
3.
Laboratory tests: Manifestation of the macroscopic torsion field in the form of phase noise of precision atomic clocks or spacetime correlations of this noise between remote laboratories.
4.
Quantum biology: The DM field is capable of entangling with quantum structures in living cells (in particular, with tubulin microtubules in the Di’osi–Penrose objective collapse model). Depending on the frequency parameters, two scenarios are realized:

  • In the absence of resonance: Torsion noise of DM causes quantum decoherence, pumping information into hidden degrees of freedom of DE and locally increasing entropy.

  • Under conditions of exact resonance: The coincidence of the DM frequency with the Penrose decoherence time frequency maintains and stabilizes quantum-coherent states inside cell organelles, protecting them from thermal noise (analogous to the radical pair mechanism in the cryptochromes of migratory birds [30]).

0.6 Mechanisms of Infrared Screening of Scalar Fields in Modified Gravity

The introduction of additional scalar degrees of freedom in the infrared (IR) limit is a common property of most modern theories of quantum gravity and modified geometry [40]. These approaches seek to explain the cosmological dynamics of Dark Energy [35,36] and the covariant phenomenology of modified Newtonian dynamics (MOND) on galactic scales [31]. However, the existence of a long-range scalar "fifth force" in the macro-world is tightly constrained by precision tests of the equivalence principle. Recent data from the MICROSCOPE space mission recorded the fulfillment of the weak equivalence principle with unprecedented accuracy — the constraint on the E"otv"os parameter is η < 10-15 [32]. To reconcile modified gravity theories with experimental data in the Solar System, an effective suppression (screening) of the scalar sector in strong fields is necessary. In modern high-energy theoretical physics, four fundamental scenarios of such IR freezing of fluctuations stand out:

0.6.1 Chameleon Mechanism (Chameleon screening)

A screening scenario proposed by J. Khoury and A. Weltman [33]. Within this approach, the suppression of the fifth force is completely controlled by the local baryonic density of the environment: the effective mass of the scalar quantum is rigidly locked to the trace of the energy-momentum tensor of matter (meff2 ∝ ρmatter). Inside dense physical media (inside planets or laboratory vacuum chambers), the field mass increases sharply, bringing the interaction radius into the ultra-short micron range (Yukawa suppression). The main conceptual flaw of the mechanism lies in its ineffectiveness in interplanetary emptiness: in the geometric vacuum of space, the baryonic density is negligible (ρ ~ 10-23 g/cm3), which should lead to the "opening" of the long-range force in Earth’s orbit, directly refuted by data from the MICROSCOPE satellite [32]. A modification of this approach is the symmetron mechanism [34], where the vacuum expectation value of the field, rather than the mass, depends on density, but it faces similar IR problems in a vacuum.

0.6.2 Dynamical Freezing (Dynamical freezing)

A global cosmological scenario investigated in detail by C. Wetterich [35] and P. Brax [36]. The freezing of scalar fluctuations here does not depend on the local environment, but occurs centrally over time as the Universe evolves. The field rolls to the bottom of a steep IR attractor of its own self-interaction potential. The conformal factor of geometry is blocked, the masses of Standard Model particles are tightly fixed at their laboratory values (electron = 0.511 MeV), and the coupling constant of the fifth force drops to zero throughout the Universe in the current epoch, fully satisfying constraints on the local run of fundamental constants.

0.6.3 Classical Vainshtein Mechanism (Vainshtein screening)

A kinetic screening scenario first proposed by A. Vainshtein [37] and developed in detail in modern theories of covariant Galileons by C. Deffayet [38] and E. Babichev [39]. Freezing of forces goes due to non-linear kinetic terms with higher derivatives of the scalar (like (∂ϕ)2□ϕ). When approaching a massive mechanical source, the spatial gradients of the scalar field itself rise sharply, because of which the non-linear kinetic operator begins to dominate over the linear one. The kinetic energy of scalar fluctuations is elastically overestimated, which effectively nullifies its coupling constant with matter baryons. The main vulnerability of the Vainshtein mechanism in continuous 4D effective field theories (EFT) is that quantum corrections often leave the scalar mass Planckian, locking the field in the microworld (10-33 cm).

0.6.4 Newtonian Freezing Mechanism (Acceleration-dependent screening)

A mathematically stable scenario capable of ensuring the burning out of scalar forces in the vacuum of planetary systems. It was covariantly formulated by S. Hossenfelder within emergent gravity [41] and investigated in non-linear models of the K-mouflage class [42]. In this 4th scenario, the non-linear kinetic operator of the scalar is driven in the equations of motion not by the gradients of the scalar field itself, but by the magnitude of the classical Newtonian acceleration of the background geometry aN (the Newtonian potential of the macroscopic source). Although there are almost no baryons in the interplanetary vacuum of the Solar System (because of which the Chameleon is powerless here), the Newtonian acceleration created by the Sun in Earth’s orbit is colossal and many orders of magnitude higher than Milgrom’s critical IR threshold (aN ≫ a0 ~ 10-10 m/c2) [31]. This strong Newtonian field of the background macrogeometry forcibly clamps the fluctuations of the emergent scalar throughout the interplanetary volume of the planetary system, driving its coupling constant to zero and seamlessly restoring Einstein’s general relativity (GR) for terrestrial laboratories and spacecraft.

0.7 Fate of Cartan Torsion Under the Newtonian Clamp

Within the independent Palatini formalism of gravity, the Newtonian freezing mechanism (the 4th scenario) possesses ideal geometric selectivity. Since it is triggered by Riemann curvature invariants, it completely burns out the scalar "fifth force" in the Solar System, preserving the stability of the local mass of the electron. However, Palatini’s algebraic connection equations with torsion do not contain Galileon derivatives, which makes the Newtonian clamp absolutely blind to Cartan torsion [43]. This allows the torsion communication channel (Weyssenhoff’s contribution) to remain active on any scale. Ordinary baryonic matter of the Earth and the Sun is spin-neutral, so macroscopic torsion is absent in the Solar System. But on the outskirts of galaxies, where the Newtonian acceleration falls below the Milgrom threshold (aN ≤ 10-10 m/s2) [31], the scalar clamp turns off. In this IR region, Dark Matter — as a coherent condensate of macroscopic spins — takes over geometry control through the open torsion channel of Cartan torsion, shaping the flat rotation curves of galaxies [31] and explaining the independent offset of hidden mass in collisionless clusters like the Bullet Cluster [44].

0.8 Quantum Many-Body Scars as Drivers of Non-Equilibrium Coherent Structures. Resolving the Tegmark Paradox via Cosmological Quantum Scars

The non-equilibrium transition from dark matter to dark energy and the structural resilience of macroscopic Bénard-like cells cannot be evaluated through standard Gibbsian thermalization. In a closed de Sitter resonator, the underlying chaotic quantum dynamics naturally yields quantum many-body scars [45] —isolated, non-ergodic periodic trajectories embedded within a formally thermalized Hilbert space spectrum. These scars act as exact topological shields against immediate quantum decoherence and thermal relaxation.Consequently, the Cartan axial current and the resulting Jacobi–Darwin geons do not dissipate into the vacuum background; instead, they are dynamically pinned to these invariant scar orbits. This mechanism bypasses the strict constraints of standard Wilsonian Effective Field Theory, allowing coherent macroscopic states—ranging from cosmological Wheeler–Misner geons to the 50-mkm biophysical networks within cellular cytoskeletons—to self-stabilize and suppress entropy generation directly through the global geometry of the closed volume.

The long-standing veto on quantum biology, formulated by Max Tegmark (2000), relies on a fundamental geometric error. Tegmark calculated the decoherence time for a "free" particle interacting with a hot, open, and expanding Friedmannian background (ΛCDM), where quantum phase information irreversibly dissipates into an infinite thermal reservoir. However, in a stationary de Sitter resonator locked within Anti-Painlevé–Gullstrand coordinates, the chaotic quantum dynamics of a closed volume operate at full capacity. The very geometry of the fixed cosmological "box" inherently generates invariant, non-ergodic periodic orbits. A living cell within the 50-μm macroscopic threshold does not need to engineer hyper-complex thermal isolation; it simply occupies these pre-existing vacuum corridors—quantum many-body scars. Inside these topological scars, the quantum phase is geometrically shielded, rendering the surrounding warm, wet environment completely powerless to destroy it. Life, therefore, emerges not as a chemical anomaly fighting thermal death, but as a self-sustaining quantum processor embedded directly into the structural architecture of the Universe.
